segments.paradiso-30-g17: Da questo passo vinto is a prepositional phrase with a past participle used adjectivally: 'da' + 'questo passo' ('from/by this passage') and 'vinto' (overcome, modifying 'passo'), i.e. 'struck/overcome by this passage.' 'mi concedo' is present indicative 'I admit/allow myself.' The clause that follows ('più che già mai ... fosse comico o tragedo') is introduced by 'più che già mai' ('more than ever before') and contains a subordinate imperfect subjunctive 'fosse' after 'che', reporting a judgment: 'that at no point of its theme it had been taken as comic or tragic' (here 'da punto di suo tema' = 'at/from any point of its subject').
segments.paradiso-30-g18: ché is the archaic/contractional conjunction meaning 'for' or 'because' (equivalent to modern perché) and here introduces the explanatory simile that follows.
segments.paradiso-30-g19: This is a simile introduced by come ('as/like'). 'sole in viso' is a metaphorical image 'the sun on (one's) face/visage' and 'che più trema' is a relative clause with present indicative 'trema' ('that trembles more' / 'that trembles most') giving a vivid, present image rather than a past-tense description.
segments.paradiso-30-g20: 'Così' ('thus/so') ties the simile to the effect described. Grammatically lo rimembrar del dolce riso is a nominalized infinitive phrase ('the remembering of the sweet smile') functioning as the subject, followed (in somewhat inverted word order) by the object/la mente mia and the verb 'scema' (present indicative of scemare, 'diminishes/lessens'). 'da me medesmo' marks separation 'from myself' or 'in me myself'; overall: 'the recollection of the sweet smile thus makes my mind diminish/withdraw from myself.'
segments.paradiso-30-g21: Dal is the contraction da + il ('from the'); 'Dal primo giorno' = 'from the first day.' 'ch’ i’ vidi' is the archaic contraction of 'che io vidi' ('that I saw'); 'il suo viso' = 'his/its face.' 'in questa vita' = 'in this life' (i.e. in the life of the world, while alive). Note the medieval orthography i’ for io and ch’ for che.
segments.paradiso-30-g22: Infino a means 'up to/until' and links the temporal span to 'questa vista' ('this vision' / 'this sight'); the comma signals continuation into the result clause that follows.
segments.paradiso-30-g23: Non m’è (non + mi + è) is an impersonal construction meaning 'it is not for me' or 'it is not possible for me'; 'il seguire al mio cantar preciso' is a nominal phrase 'to follow closely my song' (seguire governed by al 'to the'). The whole line says he cannot keep strictly to his own singing/poem.
segments.paradiso-30-g24: Ma or convien che: 'but now it is fitting that...' Convien is an impersonal verb ('it is necessary/fit') that introduces a subordinate clause with a subjunctive (here 'desista' would be expected as subjunctive, though medieval forms vary). 'Mio seguir desista' literally 'my following desist' = 'my following must desist/hold back.'
segments.paradiso-30-g25: Poetando is a gerund/participle meaning 'poetizing' or 'composing in verse' (poetare = to compose poetry); it describes the manner in which his following falls back — he will be composing, not strictly following the earlier plan.
segments.paradiso-30-g26: 'Come a l’ultimo suo ciascuno artista' is a comparative phrase: 'as every artist at his last [effort/task].' 'a l’ultimo suo' = 'at his last (moment/task)' and ciascuno artista ('each/any artist') completes the comparison; word order is typical of poetic/medieval Italian.
segments.paradiso-30-g27: Cotal qual = a poetic pairing of demonstrative (cotal, 'such') + relative/comparative qual ('as'/'which'); io lascio is present indicative 'I leave' (first person sing.). a maggior bando is a prepositional phrase 'to/for a greater band' (maggior = 'greater', bando = 'band/host' here). che introduces the comparison/relatival clause that follows (here equivalent to 'than' or 'which'). 'quel de la mia tuba' = 'that (one) of my trumpet' (quel = demonstrative; de = of; la mia tuba = 'my trumpet').
segments.paradiso-30-g28: che deduce ... terminando is a relative clause modifying 'che' in the previous segment. deduce is from dedurre and here means 'leads/brings' (transitive), not the logical 'deduce'. l’ardüa sua matera = 'its arduous matter/subject' (ardüa functioning as an adjective 'arduous' attached to matera 'subject/matter'; sua = its). terminando is a gerund/participle ('bringing to an end'/'terminating'), describing the action of deduce.
segments.paradiso-30-g29: con atto e voce = 'with a gesture/attitude and a voice' (con + two nouns in parallel). di spedito duce is an ablative/qualifying phrase 'of a spedito duce' where spedito is adjectival 'prompt/swift' and duce (here) functions as 'leader' (the lemma list gives verbal senses, but syntactically this is the leader noun). ricominciò is the main verb in past historic 'he/it began again' (ricominciò), narrating the restart of speech.
segments.paradiso-30-g30: Opening of the quoted speech. Noi siamo usciti fore = 'We have gone/come out forth' — siamo + past participle usciti forms a perfect/compound meaning 'we have gone out'; fore is an archaic/adverbial form ('outside'/'forth'). del maggior corpo al ciel = 'from/out of the greater body to the heaven' (del = of/from the; maggior corpo = 'greater body' = main body/mass). ch’è pura luce is a relative clause 'which is pure light' (ch’ = che).
segments.paradiso-30-g31: luce intellettüal is a noun phrase in apposition defining the kind of light: luce = 'light' and intellettüal is an adjective 'intellectual' (here used adjectivally to qualify the light). The diaeresis on ü is orthographic; order is noun + adjective for poetic emphasis.
segments.paradiso-30-g32: piena d’amore = adjective + prepositional phrase 'full of love' (piena = 'full'; d’ = contracted di before vowel; amore = 'love'). This is a simple predicative description of the light just named.
segments.paradiso-30-g33: amor di vero ben = genitive/partitive phrase 'love of true good' or 'love for the true good' (amor = love; di = of/for; vero modifies ben). vero is an adjective 'true', ben = 'good' (in the philosophical/theological sense: the good as an object of love).
segments.paradiso-30-g34: pien di letizia repeats the pattern 'full of joy' (pien = poetic truncated form of pieno 'full'; di letizia = 'of joy'). The truncated 'pien' is typical of Dante's meter.
segments.paradiso-30-g35: letizia che trascende ogne dolzore = 'joy that transcends every sweetness.' che introduces a defining relative clause; trascende is present indicative 'transcends/surpasses'; ogne is an archaic form of ogni 'every'; dolzore = 'sweetness' (a poetic noun). The clause explains the quality of the joy.
segments.paradiso-30-g36: Qui vederai = 'Here you will see' (vederai is future indicative of vedere). l’una e l’altra milizia = 'the one and the other host' (two opposing/paired hosts; the articles l' + una/ altra mark them). di paradiso = 'of Paradise' (genitive). The order is typical: adverb + verb + objects.
segments.paradiso-30-g37: e l’una in quelli aspetti = 'and the one in those aspects' (e = and; l'una = the one; in quelli aspetti = 'in those looks/aspects' with quelli demonstrative + plural noun). che tu vedrai a l’ultima giustizia = relative clause 'which you will see at the Last Judgment' (tu vedrai future of vedere; a l'ultima giustizia = 'at the Last Judgment' — a + article). 'aspetti' here means appearances/aspects.
segments.paradiso-30-g1: Forse is an adverb 'perhaps' modifying the whole clause. 'semilia miglia di lontano' is a distance phrase ('semilia miglia' = 'some six thousand miles', 'di lontano' = 'from far away'); word order is typical: distance phrase placed before the verb. 'ci' is an indirect-object/clitic meaning 'to/for us' and sits before the verb ferve (3rd-person singular), which here is impersonal/meteorological ('the sixth hour burns/seethes for us'). The logical subject 'l'ora sesta' follows the verb (inverted order): 'ci ferve l'ora sesta' = 'the sixth hour burns for us.'
segments.paradiso-30-g2: 'e questo mondo' is the subject phrase 'and this world'. 'china' is present indicative of chinare meaning 'bows/leans/tilts'; 'già' = 'already'. 'l'ombra' is the direct object 'the shadow'. 'quasi al letto piano' is a prepositional phrase 'almost to the level/plane of the bed' with piano functioning as an adjective ('level/flat') modifying letto.
segments.paradiso-30-g3: Quando introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. '’l mezzo del cielo' is a noun phrase 'the middle of the sky' functioning as the subject of what follows; article + noun phrase is in normal order.
segments.paradiso-30-g4: 'a noi profondo' is a dative/experiential phrase meaning 'deep to us' or 'deep as regards us' — 'a noi' ('to us') is an indirect-object phrase and profondo is an adjective agreeing with the implied subject (the middle of the sky) in sense: 'deep toward us'.
segments.paradiso-30-g5: Comincia a farsi tal = 'begins to become such'. 'comincia' is present indicative 'begins'; 'a farsi' uses the reflexive verb farsi (here 'farsi' = 'become' in a reflexive sense) with the infinitive following the preposition a. 'tal' (tale) is a reduced, archaic form meaning 'such' or 'of such a kind'.
segments.paradiso-30-g6: Ch’ (= che) introduces a result/content clause 'that'. 'alcuna stella' = 'some star' (here indefinite 'some particular star'); 'perde' is present indicative of perdere 'loses'; 'il parere' means 'its appearance/visibility' (parere used as 'appearance' rather than 'opinion'); 'infino a questo fondo' = 'even down to this depth' — infino a = 'as far as / even to' and fondo = 'depth/bottom'. The clause describes diminution of a star's apparent presence.
segments.paradiso-30-g7: E come vien = 'and as ... comes/appears' (come = 'as/like', vien = present of venire 'comes'). 'la chiarissima ancella del sol' = 'the most luminous/clear handmaid of the sun' (chiarissima superlative of chiaro). 'più oltre' = 'farther on / beyond that'. The image compares the progressive disappearance of stars with the arrival (movement) of the sun's bright handmaid (i.e., dawn's light) moving further.
segments.paradiso-30-g8: Così ... si chiude = 'thus the sky closes/closes in on itself'. '’l ciel' is the subject; 'si chiude' is a reflexive verb form meaning 'draws closed' or 'becomes closed'. 'di vista in vista' is an idiom 'from sight to sight' (i.e., from one visible band to the next), repeated noun-with-preposition to indicate stepwise progression; 'infino a la più bella' = 'up to the most beautiful' (the fairest star).
segments.paradiso-30-g9: 'Non altrimenti' is an adverbial phrase 'no differently / likewise'. 'il trïunfo' is the subject 'the triumph'; the relative clause 'che lude sempre dintorno al punto che mi vinse' modifies it: 'which plays always around the point that conquered/overcame me.' Note the embedded relative 'che mi vinse' where vinse is passato remoto (3rd sing.) 'overcame/defeated' and refers back to punto.
segments.paradiso-30-g10: Parendo is the present participle of parere 'appearing/seeming' and links back to 'il trïunfo' (the triumph appearing). 'inchiuso' is a past participle 'enclosed'; 'da quel ch’elli ’nchiude' = 'by that which it encloses' — 'quel' is demonstrative, 'ch’elli ’nchiude' a relative clause. Note archaic spellings 'elli' and 'nchiude'; 'elli' functions as a demonstrative pronoun referring to the triumph (masculine 'it').
segments.paradiso-30-g11: 'A poco a poco' is a repeated adverbial phrase meaning 'little by little'. 'al mio veder' literally 'to/for my seeing' = 'in my sight' or 'as far as I could see'. 'si stinse' is passato remoto of a verb meaning 'it died away/was extinguished' (stinse = 'was dimmed/waned'); the reflexive 'si' marks the intransitive/passive sense.
segments.paradiso-30-g12: 'Per che' = 'wherefore/therefore', introducing the result: 'tornar con li occhi a Bëatrice' is an infinitival clause 'to turn (back) with the eyes to Beatrice'. 'nulla vedere' is an infinitive phrase functioning as the object = 'to see nothing'. 'e amor mi costrinse' places the agent 'amor' (love) as subject and 'costrinse' (passato remoto) as the finite verb 'compelled/forced me' — the construction says that love forced the speaker to see nothing when he turned to Beatrice.
segments.paradiso-30-g13: Se marks the conditional protasis ('if'). 'quanto ... si dice' = 'all that is said' uses the impersonal 'si dice' (passive/impersonal 'is said') and 'infino a qui' means 'up to here' or 'to this point'. 'di lei' = 'about her'. 'fosse conchiuso' uses the imperfect subjunctive (fosse) with the past participle conchiuso: a counterfactual/hypothetical 'were enclosed'. The clause as a whole sets a hypothetical supposition.
segments.paradiso-30-g14: 'poca sarebbe' is a conditional construction ('it would be little' / 'scarcely enough'). 'a fornir' uses the preposition a + infinitive to indicate purpose or potential ('to supply/furnish'); 'questa vice' is an archaic phrase meaning 'this turn/office/duty' or 'this role/task' — the whole means 'such a single praise would be scarcely enough to perform this task.' The finite verb sarebbe is conditional of essere.
segments.paradiso-30-g15: 'La bellezza ch’io vidi' = 'the beauty that I saw' (relative clause with vidi simple past). 'si trasmoda' uses the reflexive si with trasmoda (from trasmodare) meaning 'goes beyond/exceeds/transcends'; the reflexive intensifies the intransitive sense. 'non pur di là da noi' = 'not only over there beyond us' (non pur = 'not only/even not', di là da noi = 'on the other side of us' / 'beyond our sphere').
segments.paradiso-30-g16: 'ma certo io credo' = 'but certainly I believe' (ma = but; certo = certainly; credo = present indicative 'I believe'). 'che' introduces the content clause: 'solo il suo fattor tutta la goda.' 'solo' restricts 'fattor' ('only its Maker'); 'fattor' = 'maker/creator' (archaic form); 'tutta la goda' = 'enjoys it wholly' where tutta (adverbial/quantifier) and the clitic la (direct object 'it', referring to 'la bellezza') precede the verb: goda is present subjunctive (goda) used after credo to express belief/subjectivity.
segments.paradiso-30-g77: Poi is a simple temporal adverb meaning 'then' or 'afterwards', setting this action in sequence with what came before.
segments.paradiso-30-g78: come introduces a simile 'like'; gente stata is a noun plus past participle ('people who have been/stood'), where stata describes the people as having been 'under' (sotto) larve — larve here = masks or disguises (metaphorical).
segments.paradiso-30-g79: che is a relative pronoun 'who/which'; pare is present 'seem/appear'; altro che prima is a comparative phrase meaning 'different than before', where the second che functions as 'than'.
segments.paradiso-30-g80: This subordinate clause is temporal/conditional ('se' = 'if/when') with the reflexive si plus verb sveste 'take(s) off' (here 'strip off' a disguise). la sembianza non süa = 'the appearance that was not its own', and in che disparve = 'in which it had disappeared' (che = relative 'in which'). Note: the dataset's listed sense for se does not include 'if/when', so the correct sense index is not available.
segments.paradiso-30-g81: così 'thus/so'; mi (to me) + si (reflexive/impersonal clitic) precede the verb cambiaro (archaic/metrical 3rd pl. past 'they changed'), producing a structure 'to me they changed themselves' meaning 'they appeared to me changed into'. The grammatical subject (li fiori e le faville) follows the verb, a common inversion in poetry.
segments.paradiso-30-g82: sì ch' = 'so that' (sì ... ch' introduces a result clause); io vidi is simple past 'I saw'. ambo le corti del ciel manifeste = 'both the courts of heaven manifest' (ambo = both). Note that corte here means 'court' (heavenly assemblies), and manifeste = 'made manifest/openly revealed.'
segments.paradiso-30-g83: Vocative: O is the exclamation 'O/Oh' addressing the addressee; isplendor (archaic spelling) = 'splendour/glory' and di Dio = 'of God', all in direct address.
segments.paradiso-30-g84: per cu' = 'through whom' (cu is an archaic relative 'cui'), io vidi = 'I saw' — the relative clause gives the instrument/medium (through whom the poet saw) l'alto trïunfo del regno verace = 'the high triumph of the true reign/kingdom'.
segments.paradiso-30-g85: dammi is the imperative 'give me' (dà/da' + mi), virtù = 'power' or 'ability'; a dir = 'to say/tell'; com' ïo il vidi = 'how I saw it' (com' = come 'how', ïo emphatic 'I', il = 'it' referring back to the vision). Note: the dataset's listed senses for dammi are incorrect, so the correct sense index is not available there.
segments.paradiso-30-g86: Lume è là sù = 'There is a light up there' (locative); the relative clause che visibile face lo creatore = 'that makes the Creator visible' (face is an archaic 3rd singular present 'fa' = 'makes'); a quella creatura introduces the indirect object 'to that creature', and che solo in lui vedere ha la sua pace = 'who has her peace only in seeing him' where vedere functions as a noun ('in seeing').
segments.paradiso-30-g87: E' here is an archaic subject pronoun or marker (the dataset lacks the correct sense), si distende = 'it stretches/extends itself' (reflexive), in circular figura = 'into a circular shape' (circular is an older/metrical form).
segments.paradiso-30-g88: in tanto che = 'to such an extent that'; la sua circunferenza sarebbe = 'its circumference would be' (sarebbe = conditional 'would be'); al sol troppo larga cintura = 'too wide a girdle even for the sun alone' — sol is archaic and here effectively 'sole/sun' (the provided senses do not include this meaning). The phrase is hyperbolic: the circumference so large it would not even fit as a belt around the sun.
segments.paradiso-30-g101: Two adverbial adjectives placed front for emphasis: 'Presso' (near) paired with 'lontano' (far) joined by 'e' to give an inclusive/contrasting sense (i.e. everywhere or at all distances). The phrase is elliptical and functions as an adverbial setting for what follows.
segments.paradiso-30-g102: 'lì' is a locative adverb ('there') reinforcing the adverbial phrase before it; its position after the pair serves as an intensifier (i.e. 'there/thereabouts').
segments.paradiso-30-g103: A negative correlative 'né ... né ...' = 'neither ... nor ...'. 'Pon' and 'leva' are verbal stems used nominally/elliptically (common in medieval/poetic Italian) meaning 'putting/placing' and 'lifting/removing' respectively; the colon after them marks the clause that explains why.
segments.paradiso-30-g104: 'ché' introduces the reason/cause ('for/because'). 'Dove' here is generic/relative ('wherever'). 'Dio' is the subject; 'sanza mezzo' is a prepositional phrase 'without a middle/mediator' (mezzo = middle, means); 'governa' is present indicative, 3rd person singular, stating a general truth: 'God rules'.
segments.paradiso-30-g105: Subject–predicate order: 'la legge natural' = 'the natural law' (adjective 'natural' postnominal, a poetic/older placement). 'nulla' functions as an adverbial/qualifier meaning 'nothing' / 'not at all'. 'rileva' (3rd person singular present) here means 'has weight/importance' or 'matters' — literally 'is of consequence' (Dante uses it to say natural law has no force here).
segments.paradiso-30-g106: 'Nel' is the contraction of 'in il' = 'in the'. 'giallo' is an adjective/noun 'yellow' that qualifies the following noun. 'de la rosa' = 'of the rose' (poetic 'de' for 'di'); 'sempiterna' is an adjective meaning 'everlasting' (poetic form derived from 'sempiterno'). The whole prepositional phrase marks location/part of the vision: 'in the yellow part of the everlasting rose.'
segments.paradiso-30-g107: 'che' introduces a relative clause describing the rose. 'si' is reflexive/mediopassive, used with intransitive verbs to indicate a state or arrangement. 'digrada' and 'dilata' are present indicative verbs (3rd sing.) giving a sequence of actions/states: 'is graded/steps down' and 'expands/widens'. 'redole' is also 3rd sing. and takes 'odor di lode' as its complement: 'gives off/smells like a scent of praise.' 'al sol' ('to the Sun') modifies the direction/recipient of that praise; 'che sempre verna' is a relative clause qualifying 'sol' ('which is ever vernal'). Overall, a descriptive, present-tense pictorial clause (poetic present).
segments.paradiso-30-g108: A comparative/simile introduced by 'qual' ('such as/like'): 'qual è colui che tace e dicer vole' = 'as is he who is silent and wishes to speak.' 'tace' (3rd sing. present) = 'is silent'; 'dicer' is an archaic infinitive (for 'dire') used with 'vole' (3rd sing. present of 'volere') to mean 'wants to say' or 'wishes to speak.'
segments.paradiso-30-g109: 'mi' is the clitic object 'me'. 'trasse' is passato remoto (third person singular of 'trarre') meaning 'drew' or 'pulled' (here figuratively 'led me' / 'guided me toward the sight'). 'Bëatrice' is the agent (proper name). The simple past tense narrates the poet's immediate experience in the vision.
segments.paradiso-30-g110: 'e' = 'and' links the action to what follows; 'disse' is passato remoto (3rd sing. of 'dire') 'said', introducing direct speech. Standard narrative sequencing: she drew him and said.
segments.paradiso-30-g111: Direct speech begins with the imperative 'Mira' ('Look!/Behold!') addressed to the speaker. 'quanto è 'l convento' is an exclamatory measure 'how great/large is the convent/assembly'; 'de le bianche stole' is a genitive phrase 'of the white stoles' (plural), where 'convento' here means 'gathering/assembly' rather than strictly a monastic house. The syntax is an exclamatory imperative plus a descriptive nominal clause.
segments.paradiso-30-g112: Vedi = 'See' (imperative or present used as command/imperative). nostra città = 'our city' (possessive + noun). quant’ ella gira = contracted quant' (quanto/quanto) 'how much' or 'how' + ella gira 'it turns/goes around' — quant’ is the shortened form used before a vowel; the clause comments on the city's extent or movement.
segments.paradiso-30-g113: vedi li nostri scanni = 'see our benches/seats/thrones' (li = the; nostri scanni = our seats). sì ripieni = 'so/indeed full' (sì = 'so' here, used adverbially; ripieni = past participle/adjective 'filled'). The phrase emphasizes how crowded the seats are.
segments.paradiso-30-g114: che poca gente più ci si disira = a result clause introduced by che 'so that/so that' (here: 'so that'). poca gente = 'few people'; più = 'more'/'any more' (negating desire for additional people); ci is locative/pronominal 'here/there' or 'for us'; si disira is impersonal/passive reflexive construction with disirare (present indicative 'is desired/longed for') — together meaning 'so few people are desired there any more' or 'there is little desire for more people.'
segments.paradiso-30-g115: E 'n is a contracted, poetic form of "E in" (= "And in"); 'n here introduces the location. "quel gran seggio a che tu li occhi tieni" uses an archaic relative construction "a che" = "at which/where" and places the prepositional phrase before the relative clause. "li occhi tieni" literally "you keep the eyes" = "you keep your eyes" (i.e. you fix your gaze); the object pronoun "li" is the older form for "gli". "per la corona che già v’è sù posta" explains the reason: "per" = "because of/for," "già" = "already," "v’è" = "vi è" (there is), and "sù posta" is a past-participle phrase ("placed up/on it") with the adverb "sù" before the participle — a common poetic order.
segments.paradiso-30-g116: "prima che" introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning "before"; after "che" Dante uses the present form "ceni". Grammatically this is the second-person form (tu ceni) which, in such subordinate temporal clauses, can be read as present subjunctive or indicative in older/poetic usage — here it carries the sense "before you dine (at these nuptials)". "a queste nozze" uses "a" = "at" with the noun "nozze" (nuptials).
segments.paradiso-30-g117: "sederà" is the future indicative (3rd singular) meaning "will sit" or "shall sit," placed before its subject for poetic inversion. "l’alma" is the subject in postverbal position (the soul). Together they mean "the soul will sit."
segments.paradiso-30-g118: The relative pronoun "che" introduces a clause describing the soul. "fia" is an archaic/fixed future form related to "essere" (in Dante it functions like a future "will be"); here it means "shall be/will be." "giù" = "down/below." "agosta" is an archaic/poetic form (see note) meaning "augusta/august" (i.e. exalted or crowned) and is used predicatively: "that shall be made august below."
segments.paradiso-30-g119: "de" is the older preposition for "di" meaning "of." "l’ alto Arrigo" = "the lofty/high Henry" (Arrigo = Henry); this is a genitive phrase identifying whose soul it is: "the soul of the lofty Henry." The adjective "alto" is an honorific/epithet here.
segments.paradiso-30-g120: The relative "ch'" (= "che" = "who/that") begins the clause describing Arrigo's action. "a drizzare" uses the preposition "a" + infinitive to indicate purpose or intent ("to straighten/bring upright"). "verrà" is the future indicative (3rd singular) "will come." The adverbial phrase "in prima" means "first/first of all" and is followed by the temporal clause "ch’ella sia disposta" where "sia" is the present subjunctive of "essere" (required after "prima che") and "disposta" is a past-participle used adjectivally meaning "prepared/arranged": thus, "who will come first to straighten Italy before it is prepared."
segments.paradiso-30-g89: Fassi is an archaic/pronominal passive/impersonal form (farsi) meaning 'is made' or 'becomes'; the phrase Fassi di raggio tutta sua parvenza literally places the verb first for poetic effect: 'its whole appearance is made of a ray.' Reflesso is a past participle modifying raggio ('a ray reflected') and al sommo del mobile primo is a prepositional phrase ('to/at the summit of the first movable') specifying where the ray is reflected.
segments.paradiso-30-g90: che is the relative pronoun 'which/that' introducing a clause modifying 'mobile primo'; prende is present indicative 'takes' or 'receives' and quindi here means 'thereupon/then' (a temporal/consequential connector); vivere and potenza are nouns here in a verbal phrase 'prendere vivere e potenza' meaning 'to take on life and power.'
segments.paradiso-30-g91: E come = 'and as'; clivo (nominative) is the subject 'a slope' and the clause inverts typical order for poetic effect. in acqua di suo imo is a prepositional phrase 'in the water of its deepest part' (di suo imo = 'of its lowest/deepest [part]'). si specchia is a reflexive present indicative ('mirrors itself' / 'is reflected'), used here in a general/descriptive present.
segments.paradiso-30-g92: quasi 'as if' introduces a concessive/imagined motive; per + infinitive vedersi (vedere + si) expresses purpose or appearance here and means 'to see itself' (vedersi = 'to see oneself'); addorno is an adjective/past-participle ('adorned'), so quasi per vedersi addorno = 'as if to see itself adorned.'
segments.paradiso-30-g93: quando è = 'when it is' (present tense used to describe a habitual or characteristic condition); nel verde = 'in the green' (i.e. among greenery) and ne’ fioretti opimo is an archaic contraction (ne' = in the / among the) followed by fioretti opimo = 'rich/lush little flowers.' The clause explains the circumstance in which the slope appears adorned.
segments.paradiso-30-g94: sì is an adverb here meaning 'so' or 'thus,' used to introduce the main comparative clause that follows.
segments.paradiso-30-g95: soprastando is a present participle (from soprastare) meaning 'overhanging' or 'standing over'; al lume is the object of the preposition 'to/above the light' and the repeated intorno intorno ('around and around') is a poetic doubling meaning 'all around' or 'on every side.' The whole phrase modifies the observer's position relative to the light.
segments.paradiso-30-g96: vidi is the passato remoto 'I saw'; followed by an infinitive clause specchiarsi ('to be reflected' / 'to mirror itself') in a perception construction (I saw X reflected). in più di mille soglie = 'on more than a thousand thresholds'; quanto di noi là sù fatto ha ritorno is a subordinate nominal clause meaning 'how much/of what of us up there has returned' — note the somewhat inverted perfect phrase fatto ha ritorno (literally 'has made return') = 'has returned.'
segments.paradiso-30-g97: E se = 'and if' (introducing a conditional/hypothetical); l’infimo grado is the subject 'the lowest degree/rank'; in sé = 'in itself'; raccoglie is present indicative 'gathers' or 'contains' (here meaning 'collects into itself'); sì grande lume = 'so great a light' (sì modifies grande). The clause states a hypothetical: if the lowest rank contains so much light.
segments.paradiso-30-g98: quanta è la larghezza = exclamatory/relative 'how great is the width'; di questa rosa = 'of this rose'; ne l’estreme foglie is an archaic phrasing (ne + l') meaning 'in/among the outermost leaves' (foglie = 'leaves' or 'petals' here). The whole is an exclamation comparing the small rank's light to the overall size of the Rose.
segments.paradiso-30-g99: La vista mia = 'my sight'; ne l’ampio e ne l’altezza uses the archaic ne + article ('in the') twice to mean 'in the width and in the height'; non si smarriva is an imperfect reflexive 'did not lose itself' or 'was not lost' (si smarriva = would lose itself), so the whole says the gaze did not become confused by extent or height.
segments.paradiso-30-g100: ma = 'but'; tutto prendeva is imperfect 'it took in everything' (prendere here = 'to grasp, take in' perception-wise). il quanto e ’l quale are abstract nouns (the 'how much' and the 'which/what' — i.e., the extent and the character) and di quella allegrezza = 'of that joy.' The structure treats quanto and quale as substantive, meaning 'both the amount and the quality of that delight.'
segments.paradiso-30-g121: This whole line is a single clause with a noun phrase followed by a relative clause and a result clause. 'La cieca cupidigia' is the subject (definite article + adjective + noun). 'che v’ammalia' is a relative clause: 'che' (relative pronoun) + 'v’' (archaic clitic for vi = you) + 'ammalia' (3rd-person singular present of ammaliare, poetic form meaning 'bewitch' or 'ensnare'). The remainder 'simili fatti v’ha al fantolino che muor per fame e caccia via la balia' means 'it has similar effects on the little page...' where 'v’ha' is 'ha' with clitic 'vi' (it has in/for you) and 'fatti' functions roughly as 'effects' or 'deeds'; the subordinate 'che muor per fame e caccia via la balia' modifies 'fantolino'.
segments.paradiso-30-g122: 'E' = and. 'fia' is the future of essere in Dantean/Tuscan usage (equivalent to modern 'sarà') and means 'will be'. 'prefetto' is a predicate noun meaning an appointed official; 'nel foro divino' is a prepositional phrase 'in the divine forum/court'. 'allora tal' (adverb + demonstrative) places the timing ('then') and identifies 'tal' = 'such a one' (i.e. such a person).
segments.paradiso-30-g123: 'che' introduces a relative clause modifying 'tal'. 'palese e coverto' pairs opposites (open/public and covered/secret) to mean 'whether openly or covertly'. 'non anderà' is future tense with negation ('will not go' / 'will not accompany'); 'con lui' = 'with him'; 'per un cammino' is a prepositional phrase literally 'by/along a path', here 'on the same path'.
segments.paradiso-30-g124: 'Ma' = but. 'poco poi' = shortly afterwards. 'sarà' is future 'will be'. 'da Dio sofferto' is a passive/ adjectival construction with 'da' marking the agent (by God) and 'sofferto' (past participle used adjectivally) meaning 'tolerated/allowed'. 'nel santo officio' = 'in the holy office' (the ecclesiastical post).
segments.paradiso-30-g125: The clause begins with 'ch’' (che) introducing an explanatory clause ('for'/'that'). 'el' is the archaic subject pronoun 'he'. 'sarà detruso' = future passive 'will be thrust down/ousted' (detruso = past participle of detrudere). 'là dove Simon mago è' = 'to/there where Simon Magus is'. 'per suo merto' = 'by/through his own merit/desert' (per indicating cause/reason and 'merto' = 'desert' in the sense of what one deserves).
segments.paradiso-30-g126: 'e farà' = 'and (he/it) will cause' or 'and will make'; after the causative 'farà' the person effected is given ('quel d’Alagna' = 'that one from Alagna') followed by the infinitive 'intrar' (to enter): construction = 'will cause that one from Alagna to enter'. 'più giuso' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'further down' or 'still lower'.
segments.paradiso-30-g38: This is a simile introduced by Come = 'like/as'. Sùbito is an adverb modifying lampo ('sudden flash'), lampo is the main noun; che introduces a relative clause whose verb discetti is an archaic 3rd-person form agreeing with lampo. 'Li spiriti visivi' is the direct object of discetti and means the perceiving or visual faculties ('the visual spirits').
segments.paradiso-30-g39: Sì che introduces a result clause ('so that'). Priva is a 3rd-person verb meaning 'deprives/takes away' (the flash is the implied subject); da l’atto = 'by the action' (instrumental/causal), l’occhio is the direct object; di più forti obietti = 'of stronger objects' (obietti = 'objects' or sights).
segments.paradiso-30-g40: Così = 'thus/so'. Mi is the clitic indirect object 'to me' attached to the verb; circunfulse is a poetic past-tense form (preterite) meaning 'surrounded/shone around' (3rd-person singular) and takes luce viva ('living/vivid light') as its subject. Word order places the clitic before the verb as is normal in Italian poetry.
segments.paradiso-30-g41: E = 'and'. Lasciommi is the past tense with enclitic mi ('left me' or 'left me in a state'); fasciato is a past participle used adjectivally ('wrapped') referring to the speaker; di tal velo del suo fulgor is a prepositional phrase: 'in/with such a veil of his radiance', where del = di + il.
segments.paradiso-30-g42: Che introduces the result/consequence clause ('so that/that'); nulla m’appariva uses the clitic m’ (mi) before the imperfect appariva ('was visible/appeared to me'), meaning 'nothing appeared to me' or 'I could see nothing'.
segments.paradiso-30-g43: Sempre = 'always'. L’amor is the subject and is modified by the relative clause che queta questo cielo ('that quiets/calms this heaven'). Accoglie in sé is the verb phrase 'welcomes/receives into itself' (sé here = 'itself'), and con sì fatta salute is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'with such a salvation' describing the manner.
segments.paradiso-30-g44: Per + infinitive (per far) introduces purpose: 'in order to make'. Far + past participle disposto is a causative construction meaning 'to make (something) disposed/prepared'; a sua fiamma il candelo places the indirect object/purpose (for its flame) before the direct object the candle (il candelo) as the thing being prepared.
segments.paradiso-30-g45: Non fur più tosto ... venute is an archaic 'no sooner ... than' construction: Non + (fur = past 'were') + più tosto ('sooner') locates the brief words entering 'within me' (dentro a me). Venute is the past participle agreeing with queste parole; brievi is an adjective 'brief' modifying parole.
segments.paradiso-30-g46: Ch’io compresi introduces an object clause ('that I understood/perceived'). Compresi is 1st-person past ('I perceived'); me sormontar di sopr’ a mia virtute is an infinitival phrase with me as object meaning 'my being overcome/raised above my power', di sopr’ = 'above/over', a mia virtute = 'my strength/ability'.
segments.paradiso-30-g47: E = 'and'. Di novella vista = 'by/from a new vision' (novella here functions as 'new' in poetic use); mi raccesi is the past tense 'I was/re‑kindled/awakened' with the clitic mi; tale = 'such' or 'to such a degree', linking to the following result clause.
segments.paradiso-30-g48: Che introduces a result clause: 'so that/that no light is so mere/pure'. Nulla luce è tanto mera = 'there is no light so sheer/pure', with è the present indicative 'is' and mera used in the sense 'mere/absolute/pure' (poetic).
segments.paradiso-30-g49: This subordinate clause continues the result: 'that my eyes would not have defended themselves'. Li occhi miei = 'my eyes'; non si fosser difesi uses an archaic subjunctive/conditional perfect construction (fosser = past subjunctive of essere) with the past participle difesi 'defended', expressing that the eyes would have been unable to shield themselves.
segments.paradiso-30-g50: E vidi = 'and I saw' (vidi = past historic). Lume in forma di rivera = 'a light in the form/shape of a bank/strand' (rivera here = bank/stream-edge). Fulvido di fulgore is an adjectival phrase 'glowing/bright with splendour' (fulvido + di fulgore).
segments.paradiso-30-g51: Intra due rive = 'between/among two banks' (intra is used here in the sense 'among/between'); dipinte is a past participle 'painted' modifying rive; di mirabil primavera = 'with/of wondrous spring' describing the depiction on the banks.
segments.paradiso-30-g52: Di tal fiumana = 'from such a river' (tal = such). Uscian is a poetic/archaic 3rd-person plural form meaning 'they came out/went forth'; faville vive = 'living sparks' (faville = sparks, vive = alive/lively).
segments.paradiso-30-g53: E d’ogne parte = 'and from every side'; si mettien is a reflexive 3rd-person plural imperfect/present form meaning 'they placed themselves' or 'they settled themselves'; ne’ fiori = 'into the flowers' (ne = in).
segments.paradiso-30-g54: Quasi = 'as if/like'. Rubin = 'ruby'; che oro circunscrive is a relative clause 'that/which gold circumscribes/encircles' (circunscrive is 3rd-person singular present). The simile compares the sparks in the flowers to rubies surrounded by gold.
segments.paradiso-30-g55: Poi = 'then/afterwards', a simple temporal connective linking the previous image to the following action.
segments.paradiso-30-g56: Come = 'as/like'. Inebrïate da li odori = 'as if intoxicated/enraptured by the scents' (inebrïate is a participle agreeing with the sparks), where da indicates cause/agent.
segments.paradiso-30-g57: Riprofondavan is a poetic imperfect 3rd-person plural meaning 'they plunged again' or 'they sank back down'; sé is the reflexive 'themselves'; nel miro gurge = 'into the wondrous whirl/abyss' (miro qualifying gurge as marvelous).
segments.paradiso-30-g58: E = 'and'. S’una intrava is elliptical/poetic: s’ = se/si or a clitic representing 'if/when' or reflexive 'oneself' depending on context, una = 'one', intrava = imperfect 'entered' (3rd-singular). Here the sense is 'and when one entered'.
segments.paradiso-30-g59: Un’altra = 'another' (feminine, referring to another spark/one). N’uscia fori is an archaic phrasing meaning 'another went out' or 'came out' (n’ is a contraction indicating movement away/therefrom, uscia here is the poetic past/imperfect form of uscire, fori = 'out/outside'), so the clause gives the alternating movement 'if one entered, another went out'.
segments.paradiso-30-g60: L’ (elided il) + alto disio: the definite article is elided before a vowel (L’ = il). alto disio is a noun phrase meaning “lofty desire.” che introduces a relative clause modifying disio; mo is an archaic adverb meaning “now,” t’ is the clitic object “you,” and infiamma e urge are two present-tense verbs (3rd person singular) that together describe what the desire does (“inflames and urges”).
segments.paradiso-30-g61: d’aver is the contracted form of di avere and introduces an infinitival purpose/contents: “to have.” notizia di ciò che tu vei is an object phrase: notizia (piece of information/knowledge) of ciò che (that which) tu vei (vei = thou/you see; archaic present), so the whole says the desire is to have knowledge of what you are seeing.
segments.paradiso-30-g62: This line uses a correlative comparative: tanto ... quanto (here compressed) to mean “the more ... the more.” mi piace più = “pleases me more”; quanto più turge = “the more it swells.” turge is an archaic present (3rd singular) meaning “swells.” The word order places the evaluator (mi) before the verb piacere as usual.
segments.paradiso-30-g63: ma introduces a contrast: “but.” di quest’ acqua = “of this water” (quest’ = questa elided). convien is an impersonal present meaning “it is necessary/fit,” followed by che + a finite clause introducing the obligative content: tu bei is an archaic form meaning “you drink” (here used to express necessity: “you must drink”). prima che introduces a temporal clause “before” + si sazi (si + sazi = reflexive/middle voice ‘become satisfied’), so the clause says you must drink this water before such thirst is satisfied in you.
segments.paradiso-30-g64: così mi disse is a simple past (passato remoto) “thus said to me.” il sol de li occhi miei is a poetic epithet: sol here functions as a noun in apposition (the ‘sun’/radiant source) + de li (archaic of the) occhi miei (my eyes). The whole tags the preceding quoted speech as coming from that luminous source.
segments.paradiso-30-g65: Anche is an adverb meaning “also/even.” soggiunse is passato remoto (3rd sing.) of soggiungere, “he/she/it added (said),” introducing another explanatory remark; the colon that follows begins the direct quotation.
segments.paradiso-30-g66: A coordinated subject: Il fiume e li topazi ... e ’l rider de l’erbe — three items joined by e. ch’entrano ed escono is a relative clause modifying topazi (topazes that enter and exit). son is the present plural of essere ‘are.’ di lor vero is a postpositive phrase meaning “truly/of them,” while umbriferi prefazi is a descriptive noun phrase: umbriferi (literally “shade-bearing”) modifying prefazi (“prefaces”/preludes). Note the archaic plural article li and the contracted ch’ for che.
segments.paradiso-30-g67: Non che introduces a corrective clause meaning “not that …” da sé = “by themselves/in themselves.” sian is the present subjunctive (siano) used after this kind of negative concessive phrasing: “it is not that these things are ….” queste cose acerbe is the predicative phrase; acerbe is an adjective (‘harsh/unripe’) describing cose.
segments.paradiso-30-g68: ma introduces the contrast: “but.” è difetto here means literally “there is a defect/fault”; da la parte tua is an idiom meaning “on your part” (i.e. the fault lies with you). The verb è is the simple present ‘is.’
segments.paradiso-30-g69: che introduces an explanatory clause “that …” non hai viste is a compound past construction (hai + past participle viste) meaning “you have not seen” with the participle agreeing in gender/number with the feminine plural (queste cose). ancor = still/yet; tanto superbe = “so splendid/lofty” (superbe agrees with cose).
segments.paradiso-30-g70: Non è fantin che ... is a negative-existential comparative: “There is no child who …” fantin is an archaic diminutive meaning “child/lad.” sì sùbito rua: sì sùbito = “so/so suddenly”; rua is an archaic verb here meaning “rush” (Dante’s usage), and col volto verso il latte is an adverbial phrase “with his face toward the milk.”
segments.paradiso-30-g71: se si svegli = “if he wakes” (si is reflexive/middle); molto tardato da l’usanza sua = “very late from his usual custom” (molto tardato = considerably late; da l’usanza sua = later than his usual habit). The clause qualifies the condition under which a child would rush to milk; Dante compares himself to such a late-awakening child.
segments.paradiso-30-g72: come fec’ io is a brief past: fec’ is the elided passato remoto of fare (feci) and io is emphatic “as I did.” The short clause draws the simile between the speaker’s action and that sudden child.
segments.paradiso-30-g73: per far introduces purpose: “in order to make.” migliori spegli = “better mirrors/reflections” (spegli = poetic/archaic for miroir/reflection), ancor de li occhi = “still/of the eyes” (i.e. still better mirrors of my eyes). The infinitive far governs the noun phrase that follows as its object/purpose.
segments.paradiso-30-g74: chinandomi is a gerund (“bending myself”/“leaning down”), reflexive with the clitic -mi. a l’onda = “to the wave” and che si deriva is a relative clause “that flows/derives” (deriva = flows). perché vi s’immegli is a purpose clause: perché = “so that,” vi = “there/in it,” s’ (reflexive) + immegli (archaic comparative/adverb meaning “become better” or “be better”), so: “so that there it may be improved.”
segments.paradiso-30-g75: e sì come = “and just as.” di lei bevve: bevve is passato remoto of bere ‘drank’; di lei (‘of it’) refers to the water/wave, so “the rim/edge drank of it.” la gronda de le palpebre mie = “the brim/rim of my eyelids” (gronda here = brim/edge/drip). The phrase is metaphorical: the eyelids’ edge takes up the water.
segments.paradiso-30-g76: così mi parve = “so it seemed to me” (parve = passato remoto of parere). di sua lunghezza divenuta tonda is a predicative phrase: di sua lunghezza (“of its length/extent”), divenuta (feminine past participle agreeing with lunghezza) = “having become,” tonda = adjective “round.” The clause means the thing’s length seemed to have become rounded.

sentences.paradiso-30-s5: Overcome by this passage, I admit to myself more than ever that at no point in its subject was it to be taken as comic or tragic: for, as the sun on a face that trembles most, so the remembrance of the sweet smile makes my mind shrink away from myself.
sentences.paradiso-30-s6: From the first day that I saw his face in this life until this sight, it is not for me to follow my song exactly; but now it is fitting that my following give way and fall further behind his beauty, composing in verse, as every artist does at his final effort.
sentences.paradiso-30-s7: Such as I leave to a larger band than that of my trumpet, which, bringing its arduous subject to an end, with the bearing and voice of a swift leader began again: "We have gone forth outside the greater body into the heaven which is pure light: intellectual light, full of love; love of the true good, full of joy; joy that transcends every sweetness."
sentences.paradiso-30-s8: Here you will see both the one and the other host of Paradise, and the one in those aspects that you will see at the Last Judgment.
sentences.paradiso-30-s1: Perhaps some six thousand miles away the sixth hour burns for us, and this world already bows the shadow almost down to the level of the bed, when the middle of the sky, deep toward us, begins to take on such a state that some star loses its appearance even to this depth; and as the sun's most luminous handmaid comes farther onward, so the sky closes itself, from sight to sight, up to the most beautiful.
sentences.paradiso-30-s2: No differently was the triumph that plays always around the point that overcame me, appearing enclosed by what it encloses; little by little it faded from my sight: therefore, when I turned my eyes to Beatrice, love forced me to see nothing.
sentences.paradiso-30-s3: If all that has been said of her up to this point could be enclosed entirely in a single praise, it would be far too little to furnish this turn/offering.
sentences.paradiso-30-s4: The beauty that I saw transcends not only beyond us, but certainly I believe that only its Maker wholly enjoys it.
sentences.paradiso-30-s17: Then, like people who have been under masks — who seem other than they were before when they take off a guise that was not their own in which they had vanished — so the flowers and the sparks were changed into greater festivals for me, so that I saw both courts of heaven laid bare.
sentences.paradiso-30-s18: O splendour of God, through whom I saw the high triumph of the true reign, give me the power to tell how I saw it!
sentences.paradiso-30-s19: There is a light up there that makes the Creator visible to that creature who has her peace only in seeing him.
sentences.paradiso-30-s20: It spreads itself out into a circular form, to such an extent that its circumference would be, as a belt, too wide even for the sun alone.
sentences.paradiso-30-s25: Near and far, there — neither putting nor lifting: for wherever God rules without a middle, natural law avails nothing.
sentences.paradiso-30-s26: In the yellow of the everlasting rose, which is arranged in graded tiers and expands and gives forth the scent of praise toward the Sun that is ever vernal, as one who is silent yet wishes to speak, Beatrice drew me and said: "Look how great is the assembly of the white stoles!"
sentences.paradiso-30-s27: See how our city turns; see our seats so full that there is little desire for more people here.
sentences.paradiso-30-s28: And on that great seat where you keep your eyes fixed because of the crown already placed upon it, before you dine at these nuptials, will sit the soul that shall be made august below, of the lofty Henry, who will come first to straighten Italy before it is prepared.
sentences.paradiso-30-s21: All its appearance was made of a ray reflected to the summit of the first movable, which thereby takes on life and power.
sentences.paradiso-30-s22: And as a slope mirrors itself in the water of its deepest part, as if to see itself adorned when it is in the green and among the rich little flowers, so—overhanging the light all around—I saw reflected on more than a thousand thresholds how much of us up there had returned.
sentences.paradiso-30-s23: And if the lowest degree gathers so great a light within itself, how great is the width of this rose at its outermost leaves!
sentences.paradiso-30-s24: My sight was not lost in the breadth nor in the height, but it took in everything — the amount and the nature — of that joy.
sentences.paradiso-30-s29: The blind covetousness that has bewitched you produces similar effects on the little page who dies of hunger and drives away the wet-nurse.
sentences.paradiso-30-s30: And then such a one will be appointed prefect in the divine forum who, openly or secretly, will not accompany him on the same path.
sentences.paradiso-30-s31: But soon after he will be little tolerated by God in the holy office; for he will be thrust down to where Simon Magus is because of his deserts, and the man from Alagna will cause him to enter still lower.
sentences.paradiso-30-s9: Like a sudden flash that disperses the visual spirits — so that by its action it robs the eye of stronger objects — thus a living light surrounded me, and left me wrapped in such a veil of its brightness that nothing appeared to me.
sentences.paradiso-30-s10: Always the love that calms this heaven receives into itself, with such salvation, in order to make the candle ready for its flame.
sentences.paradiso-30-s11: No sooner had these brief words come into me than I perceived myself lifted above my power; and by a new vision I was rekindled in such a way that there is no light so pure that my eyes would not have to defend themselves; and I saw a light in the form of a bank, glowing with radiance between two banks painted with wondrous spring.
sentences.paradiso-30-s12: From that river came living sparks, and they settled into the flowers on every side, like rubies that gold surrounds; then, as if intoxicated by the scents, they plunged again into the wondrous whirl, and when one entered, another went out.
sentences.paradiso-30-s13: “The lofty desire that now inflames and urges you to have knowledge of what you see pleases me all the more the more it swells; but of this water you must drink first, before so great a thirst is satisfied in you,” thus the sun of my eyes said to me.
sentences.paradiso-30-s14: He added also: “The river and the topazes that enter and go out, and the laughter of the grasses, are in truth their shade-bearing prefaces.
sentences.paradiso-30-s15: Not that these things are harsh by themselves; but the fault is on your side, for you have not yet seen things so lofty.”
sentences.paradiso-30-s16: No child so suddenly rushes with his face toward the milk, if he wakes much later than his usual time, as I did — that is, to make still better mirrors of my eyes, bending down to the wave that flows there so they might be improved; and just as the rim of my eyelids drank of it, so it seemed to me that its length had become round.
